summary: Nair responds to CDC framing data mining as hypothesis-generating and not, by itself, evidence of causation.
body: Nair responds to the CDC official, incorporating many of Menschik's points that appear to downplay FDA's reliance on EB data mining.[^fn-239] Nair writes, "Results from data mining are considered hypothesis generating and do not, by themselves, demonstrate causal associations."[^fn-240] He adds that little can be attained from the presence or absence of a safety signal, and notes that even the use of the terms "signal and/or safety signal have certain connotations and may trigger actions so we try not [sic] conflate data mining alerts with signals."[^fn-241]
